nativeRange.habitat: Found across turbid inner lagoons, reef flats, and clear seaward rocky and coral reefs. Zanclus cornutus is benthopelagic (living and feeding near the bottom while also swimming in open water) and is most often seen moving through the reef in small groups of two or three, though larger schools occasionally form.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with renewable sponge growth

diet.notes: In the wild, the Moorish Idol is a dedicated grazer of encrusting sponges, supplemented by small invertebrates and algae. This specialized diet is the central husbandry problem in captivity: substitute foods rarely sustain captive specimens long-term. Aquarists who attempt the species are generally advised to offer mature live rock with active sponge growth alongside finely chopped meaty foods, mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, nori, and Spirulina-based foods — fed multiple small portions per day. Ideally, collected live sponge is also offered. Once a Moorish Idol stops feeding it tends not to resume.

avoidWith: stony and soft corals (will be nipped)

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 4344x2896. Tack-sharp in-situ reef shot of a live vibrant adult showing all diagnostic features: bold black/white/yellow vertical bands, complete graceful whip-like dorsal filament fully in frame, and yellow tubular snout. Best striking pick.

Diego Delso, 5722x3815. Sharp side profile against clean teal water; intensely vibrant yellow banding and full compressed disc body. Filament trails just off the top edge. Opposite-facing angle for variety.
Philippe Bourjon, 1200x872. Sharp front three-quarter angle over coral reef; complete filament and full body visible, clearly showing snout and bands. Different perspective for gallery variety.

summary: The Moorish Idol is one of the most visually recognizable reef fish in the world and one of the most difficult to sustain in captivity. Long-term survival in home aquaria is estimated under 10%, and the species is generally regarded as suitable only for public aquariums.
careNotes:
ETHICAL ADVISORY: The Moorish Idol is one of the most poorly-suited reef fish for home aquaria ever popularised by the trade. One long-term study tracked 382 specimens over nearly three years and found zero long-term survivors; the most successful individual keeper documented achieved only a one-in-seven success ratio. Overall long-term (more than one year) survival in home tanks is estimated at well under 10%. Most specimens die within days to a few months of acquisition. Wild-caught collection continues with negligible captive recruitment to offset the loss, and the species is strongly discouraged for home purchase.

The core problem is dietary: in the wild, Zanclus cornutus grazes encrusting sponges across wide stretches of reef, behaving as an obligate-leaning spongivore. Captive substitutes rarely sustain the species, and once a specimen stops feeding it usually does not resume — wasting away over weeks even after an apparently good initial acclimation. Specimens are also extremely sensitive to transport stress, copper-based prophylactic medications used in the supply chain, reduced-salinity fish-store holding, and minor temperature excursions.

If — against advice — the species is attempted, requirements are extreme: a mature, fully-established reef system (at least one year old, ideally larger) with substantial mature live rock carrying renewable sponge growth, near-natural seawater parameters, very high water movement (~20× turnover/hour), deep front-to-back swimming space, and a minimum tank footprint on the order of 200 US gal (~750 L) for juveniles or 400+ US gal (~1500 L) for adults.

Not a tang: despite the resemblance, Zanclidae is a monotypic family (only one species, Z. cornutus) and is more closely related to surgeonfishes than to butterflyfishes. It lacks the caudal-peduncle scalpel (the retractable spine near the tail base) found in surgeonfishes and tangs. The Moorish Idol is frequently confused in the trade with the Schooling Bannerfish (Heniochus diphreutes) and Longfin Bannerfish (H. acuminatus), both of which are far more tractable in aquaria and are generally considered the responsible alternative for hobbyists drawn to the look.
breedingNotes: The Moorish Idol has not been bred in captivity. The species spawns pelagic (open-water) eggs that hatch into an unusually long-lived larval stage — an acronurus-like larva that spends months adrift at sea before settling. This extended larval duration is thought to help wild populations remain stable despite collection pressure, but it has also made aquaculture attempts unsuccessful.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Designated primary source for fish-saltwater slice. LiveAquaria's species page is explicitly labelled 'EXPERT ONLY' and recommends against home keeping for all but the most expert hobbyists. Initial fetch of /product/483/moorish-idol returned 404; the canonical product URL is /products/moorish-idol-expert-only.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Zanclidae, order Acanthuriformes), maximum size (23 cm TL), depth range (0-182 m), temperature range (24-28°C), and Indo-Pacific + Eastern Pacific distribution. FishBase notes: 'popular aquarium fish but rarely survives in tanks.'

additional.name: Tropical Fish Hobbyist Magazine
additional.notes: Bob Goemans long-form article documenting the species' captive mortality crisis: 382 specimens tracked over three years with zero long-term survivors, one-in-seven success ratio cited as best on record, <10% one-year survival overall. Source of the ethical advisory, the 200/400 US gal tank-size recommendation, and the 20x turnover guidance. Explicitly framed as 'a recommendation against keeping this fish, period.'

fish.breedingNotes: No captive spawning or rearing of the Moorish Idol has been documented. In the wild, the species releases pelagic eggs — eggs that float and develop in open water — which hatch into an unusually long-lived larval stage known as an acronurus-like larva. This larva spends months drifting at sea before eventually settling onto the reef. That extended open-water larval phase has so far made aquaculture attempts unsuccessful.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Documented captive mortality approaches 100% in home aquaria — long-term (>1 year) survival is estimated under 10% across all skill levels, and the species' obligate spongivorous diet cannot be reliably replicated outside large institutional systems. Hundreds to thousands are wild-collected each year with negligible captive recruitment. AquaMate strongly recommends that hobbyists drawn to the look choose a Heniochus bannerfish (the 'False Moorish Idol') instead and leave Zanclus cornutus to public aquaria and the reef.
